sentences.text: Well, as I mentioned before, after Schindler's, Schindler's speech on May 7-- I think it materialized exactly that way.
He has left the camp.
On May 8, in the morning, the camp gates were opened.
The Germans were gone, and we, we, we, the 28-- I think it's 28-- people have taken over the guarding of the camp.
We-- the camp itself was located on the site of a rather main highway.
And we were approximately 100 kilometers east of the American front and about 100 kilometers west of the Russian front.
We were actually in no-man's land.
We found out from people who were free, who knew what things are going, going on.
I, I, I didn't know how I knew that.
Now I know it, because probably somebody told me.
And this is why it took several days before anyone from the-- either side would have come to our camp.
The person who came actually was a Russian soldier on a horse, and very haggard.
And told us, you are now free.
And I think he spent, spent few minutes, like an hour maybe, and left.
He told us that eventually, administration will come and they will take care of the camp, of the people of the camp.
Prepare us for repatriation and back to Poland.

Since we were on the, on the side of the main road, and since the Germans have capitulated-- and they did not want to fall into the Russian hands, they preferred to fall into American hands-- they started, on the-- in large groups, walking from the east to west towards the American lines, to fall in American POW camps.
They had to pass in front of our camp.
We put up the machine gun on the, on the roof of the camp, and we put out three guys with rifles on each side of the road.
We stopped them all.
We took off their uniforms, took away, took away their weapons, and send them to the Americans in their underwear by the hundreds.
The weapons we stored in a room, in one of the factory rooms.
And we took hundreds upon hundreds of very small weapons.
They were not carrying any bigger weapons.
Unfortunately, some-- there was a situation where a group of soldiers on horses were galloping from east to west.
And people who-- other people in the camp told that they probably are the Russians.
And they started running towards them to greet them.
But these were Germans, and they started shooting and killed a few of us-- of our people.
The Germans most likely got scared, because it was already after the war.
And theoretically, they were not supposed to have any weapons anymore, by the, by the rules of the capitulation.
But unfortunately, this happened.
We also lost a few people who ate food which was left by Schindler in his warehouse, consisting of very fat pork in cans.
And they ate it.
They died.
They couldn't-- their stomachs couldn't take it.
After all these years of starvation, they could not take this kind of food.
We had a guard at the warehouse, because whoever was in charge of our group must have been smart enough to realize that this can happen.
But somehow, they managed to get the cans of the-- canned fat pork.
And unfortunately, a few of them died.
Oh, he left.
And we were in charge of that.
And we were just running the camp like the camp was run before.
We were distributing food.
All the 28 people, maybe they got some additional help-- I don't remember.
And everybody was within the camp.
Very few people dared to get out.
You have to remember that we were close to Sudetenland, which was actually a German population.
And we were kind of afraid of going in there.
We did go there eventually, in a very strong force, when the Russians arrived.
Eventually, the Russian administration came in.
They registered everybody.
They started to make preparations for repatriation to Poland, to Cracow.
But this took several weeks.
And in the meantime, we got acquainted with some of the Russian officers.
One of them was a Jewish lieutenant.

And he, and several people-- I never was part of it, unfortunately, I wish I would be-- I would have been-- they went into the Sudetenland villages and they got into the German homes and simply took away whatever they thought would be needed by our people.
This is how I got my first suit.
Two sizes oversized, but it was my first-- I didn't-- I did not have to wear my striped suit anymore.
Eventually, some weeks after that-- after we had distributed the fabrics to the-- to everybody and everything else-- a train arrived.
We were loaded up into the train and taken to Cracow.
Since most of the railroad tracks were broken, we knew that it would take several days, even though it wasn't such a great, long distance.
But we equipped all the wagons with-- this was all transport railroad-- we equipped it.
We took our bunk beds in them, put them in.
And we took little stove, iron stove, and then we cooked.
We had-- we were-- we, of course-- because we already had girlfriends and boyfriends.
And I was part of a group of about eight or nine people.
Four, five boys, and four, five girls, something like that.
And eventually we arrived in Cracow.
We were put up in a Jewish university-- not university-- in, in a Jewish dormitory of the university students from before the war.
This was a building.
In Cracow.
And we were assigned rooms there.
This is where we lived.
Unfortunately, the Russians lived there before we came.
And as we came in, as you opened the doors, the doors opened in, into the room.
So when you opened the door, you actually were hiding the wall behind it, and the Russians used it as a toilet.
They were doing business right there.
They slept there, too.
So we had to clean all that out before we could use it.
So you arrived to Cracow, and--
Arrived in Cracow.
There was a Jewish community in Cracow, which everyone now was trying to go to Jewish community, which had received, day in, day out, lists from different concentration camp-- to a list of survivors.
And everybody was looking of who survived from my family.
Unfortunately, no one, no one survived from mine.
And no one survived for most other friends who we were with.
So we decided, we don't wish to live under communist rule, and we will go out of Poland.
I went back to the house.
I, I was not allowed to go into apartments.
Somebody lived there.
I did go into the house, into the building, but I was not allowed to go to the apartment.
But it wasn't important.
There was nothing of interest anyway.
No.
I heard-- I did not hear of my sister specifically.
I knew that my sister went to Stutthof before I went to Schindler's.
I-- in-- on the-- in the Jewish community in Cracow, I found out what happened to the people in Stutthof.
Therefore, the assumption I had to make that if this happened to all the people in Stutthof, it happened to my sister as well.
The same way.
People who survived Gusen told me that he was killed.
He died.
They didn't kill him, actually.
He died of starvation.
So you decide to leave Cracow.
So the group which we had decide to leave Cracow.
And we went-- we smuggled ourselves through Czechoslovakia into Austria.
And we lived in Austria-- oh, they remained for longer than I did.
I lived in Austria until January 1946.
Oh, yeah.
There was-- we had to go over across borders.
It was not just free to go.
But we finally-- when we arrived at the Austrian border, and we were called by an American soldier laying, he was laying in the grass-- because it was American zone of occupied Austria at that point.
And the American soldier with a rifle was, was walking us.
We were, at that time, maybe eight of us, walking us towards the headquarters, where they're going to interview or interrogate us.
But in the process of walking-- I was walking next to him-- he took out the mezuzah.
And asked him, do you what that is?
I didn't, I didn't have one.
He said, sure.
That's the mezuzah.
So he let us go.
So what did you do in Austria?
[CLEARS THROAT]
We didn't do anything.
We lived in Ebensee.
This was the camp next to a forest, which, at one time, the buildings were occupied by German soldiers.
And we were allowed to move in there.
And each one of us, or the two of us, got a room.
And we lived there.
We were-- we were supplied food by the UNRRA, United Nations Rehabilitation, or Reparation Authority-- whoever the abbreviation stands for.
And we, we stayed there.
We were-- eventually, I-- eventually, we started doing business by me going back and forth to Poland.
I, again, became a smuggler.
And I took to Poland anything and everything I could find in Austria-- buy, steal, or what have you.
You see, we had a mentality at that time that we are unusual species.
We have the right to do whatever we want, and nobody can stop us.
And we did, for a while.
Eventually, they stopped us, of course.
I carried German money to Poland.
No, I'm sorry.
German money back from Poland.
I carried different merchandise to Poland, sold it there for-- and-- but I only wanted to sell it for German money, because German money-- they had a lot of German money there.
And Germany was still a, a currency in Austria at that time.
Eventually, we changed that into American script.
Script were the dollars which the soldiers-- American soldiers-- received, which-- they were not a currency-- it was not a currency which was valid in the United States.
It was an American currency valid in Europe.
They were in dollar value, valuations, but they were known as script.
But eventually, you managed to get script.
The soldiers could write to change so many script per month.
We, we made deals with them.
We gave them the script, they gave us regular money, or whatever.
So when I came to the United States, I had some money.
In January, I believe, 1946-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No.
While I was still in Austria, my future wife, who was in Bergen-Belsen, came looking for me.
And she found out somehow.
No, she went to Cracow.
Yeah.
She went to Cracow.
She found out that I went-- that I was there, that I went to Austria.
She went to Austria.
But would you believe that she went to Austria and she didn't find me there?
She found my friends.
I was on the way to Cracow.
Yeah.
She was a girlfriend.
During the stay on the airport, that's where we met.
I did not know her before the war.
We didn't know each other before the war.
Eventually, I, I was going to Cracow when she was coming to Austria.
My friends, who are, who are my partners, gave her some money.
And she said, in that case, I'm going to go back to Cracow.
And she went back to Cracow.
And would you believe, I went back to Austria?
So she was now-- first she was in Austria, I was in Cracow.
And then she was in Cracow, and I was in Austria.
So I decided, the following day, I'm going back to Cracow.
I went back to Cracow, and she was there.
I made sure that it won't wait too long, or she may decide to go back to Austria.
I went back to Cracow, and she was there.
And she lived on-- she was living, hiding, in the Jewish cemetery.
There was a building in the Jewish cemetery, and she knew somebody who was at one time related to that activity there.
And she was hiding there.
And there was a pogrom in Cracow, and they were shooting at them, but they survived.
When I came to Cracow, it was-- every time I went over the borders, it was more and more difficult to do that.
And people who wanted to get out from the Communist rule found out that Joe is there.
And Joe is a specialist already.
He already went so many times over the border-- you have to go with Joe!
Not going to go by themselves.
They are afraid now.
So Joe had 16 people to take across to Austria.
And we went across the Polish border.
There was no problem.
And it went-- we went through-- we [? went ?] [? to ?] Slovakia.
We stayed in the town Budejovice on the Austrian border, hoping that we can find a way to get across the border.
But it was very-- more and more difficult.
It was very difficult.
But there was a train coming in from Austria with many, many cars.
And there was-- it was not passenger trains-- who was bringing repatriated people from Germany into Czechoslovakia.
And the train stopped in Budejovice and unloaded.
And the train was escorted by American soldiers.
Fortunately, we had a girl in these 16 people who spoke a little bit English, and she started talking to them.
And they allowed us to hide in one of the cars.
And he says, this train is going back-- going back to Austria.
So we hid in one of those cars.
And eventually the following morning, the train started moving and it moved most of the day.
Late in the evening-- it was dark already-- the train reached the border of Austria, and the train stops.
And the Russian soldiers, with their big lantern, opening every door and looking in.
And while they were looking in, they found nobody.
So I hoped that they're not going to come to our train.
Maybe they get tired of it.
But they did, and they saw us.
And there were about six different military with their rifles like that.
We are spies.
[NON-ENGLISH] I spoke and understood a little Russian at that time.
We are spies.
And one of them jumped into-- I think it was like a sergeant-- and I was prepared for him.
I have to now backtrack a little bit.

When we delivered all these weapons which we took from the Germans and stored them in Schindler's camp after the war, we got-- 18 of us-- 28 of us, got a special paper, handwritten by a Russian general who was in charge of the Ukrainian front.
And on the bottom of it was a very peculiar triangular stamp.
And the stamp said, Kommandant of the Ukrainian Front.
When that guy came into the thing, I handed to him two bottles of whiskey, and I showed him this paper.
I had that paper.
And he started yelling at the others, I have a pass-- that the guy has a pass.
He stuck the bottles of whiskey into his pants and walked out and told them, he's okay.
They have a pass.
They can go.
So, and, and another way to be lucky.
We eventually wound up, wound up in Russia-- I'm sorry, I'm sorry.
I'm sorry, I know the camera is going.
We wound up in Austria.

And this is where I lived until January, 1946, at which time my future wife and I and the boy I mentioned before went-- who was hiding in the latrine-- went to Bergen-Belsen, where we lived until we came to the United States.
Yes.
When I-- on my last trip to Poland, where I took the 16 people back out, he was in the-- in one of them.
He-- at that time, he was operated on his middle ear.
And he was sent by whoever the medical profession to a, like, a sanitarium, which would be like a, like a convalescent home out of Warsaw.
And I-- when I travelled to Poland, smuggled, I always had an assistant.
I said the assistant-- I paid him an airplane ticket for helping to go there and bring the, the kid back.
He was now, at that time, eight years old.
And he was actually my future wife's cousin.
This is how he's my cousin, you know, my blood cousin.
And he was one of the 16 people I smuggled back to Austria.
That was my last trip.
I never came back.
Never went back.
Because my future wife's mother was still there.
She survived.
There was a-- a camp, a DP camp-- very large.
At one time, if I am correct, there were about 40,000 people there.
And we lived from day to day.
They distributed food, they distributed some clothing.
But this, of course, wasn't enough for a person as active as I am.
So I had to do some business.
I started traveling to Hanover first, because it was closest-- about 35, 40 kilometers.
Then to Hamburg, which was about 100 kilometers.
And then to Cologne, which was about 350 kilometers.
And started doing business.
You name it, I bought.
You name it, I sold.
[LAUGHS] Eventually, this came to an end.
The Germans became more and more powerful and in control of the areas.
And this thing was very difficult to, to proceed with.
So I decided that I am going to go into business roasting coffee in the, in the DP camp.
And my last visit, my last trip to the, to Cologne, I bought a used roasting machine.
But it was a big one.
You could put in a whole sack of coffee, of green coffee, and roast it.
And we were in a protected area.
It was illegal.
The protected area was because there was a DP camp.
The German police had no authority.
This was in English zone of, of German occupation.
And the, the English had the authority on it, but they were very, very lenient.
So we set up in the attic of one of the buildings, roasting coffee factory.
And we started roasting it.
But it wasn't by myself.
There was already-- there were already other people who did the same thing.
And as a matter of fact, there was a group of Romanian Jewish people who were roasting it on a very large scale.
And I only had one machine.
They had about a dozen machines.
The Germans are coming every morning and buying the coffee-- I mean the German civilians-- by the sack.
We did only sold it by the sack.
I think 40 kilograms, or whatever.
And eventually, we went into competition with the big group.
We were a very small group.
And they offered us partnership so we will be stopped.
[LAUGHTER]
So we stopped, and we got in partnership.
And this went on for some time, and we made some money.
Eventually, we immigrated to the United States, and we took the money with us.
[LAUGHS] When did you get married?
I got married April 8, I believe, in 1948.
I had a daughter in Germany, who was born September 20th, 1949.
I came to the United States December 21st, 1949.
My daughter was three months old.
We were fortunate.
We were allowed to fly to the United States rather than go by boat, because we had a three-month-old baby.
Everybody else had to go on the, on the transport ships, which was a hell of a job of 11 days.
Yes.
It was my first choice.
I had three choices.
I had at the time-- and I never mentioned him-- a cousin who was from Cracow, eventually wound up through Lithuania in, in Japan, and eventually in Australia.
When we were in ghetto, we got from Red Cross-- no, yeah, that's right.
When we were in ghetto, we got from Red Cross a package and a letter from him.
He was in Kobe, Japan.
And he said-- in the letter, he said, within a couple weeks, I will be going to Australia.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
He was the only one, sort of surviving cousin that I knew of.
There were others, but I did not know of them at that time.
My choice-- sorry.
Oh, that's right.
So he sent me documents to go to Australia, but they were only for one person.
At that point, I was so involved with my future wife that I said, unless you can give it to me for two, I don't want to go.
But he couldn't-- supposedly couldn't do it for two.
I don't know the details.
Maybe we would have married at that point, maybe he could, maybe he could have given it to us, but wouldn't.
Then, of course, I could have gone to Israel.
I sent out many people to Israel, but I went to the United States.
I worked very hard on the illegal aliyah, some of-- with the Jewish brigade-- if you're familiar with.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some with some other Zionist organizations.
But when the time came to go, I said, I'm going to the United States.
Well, we-- first, we organized things for the people to get things for them so they can take with them.
Because most people were poor.
Not everybody was active enough, like I was, to make money, for instance.
People lived day to day from the soup they were delivered.
Also, we have helped a lot Palestine Haganah.
We were buying weapons and vehicles from American and English soldiers-- I mean groups.
And we were packing them into boxes and shipping them as agricultural equipment to Palestine, through Cyprus, Cyprus to Greece.
So this is what I was involved in.
We were collecting money from people who-- we, we being in business, knew who is big in business.
So we knew who to squeeze for money.
We squeezed them for money, and we, we were buying.
There were-- you can't imagine what was going on.
Across the border of them-- of the English, American zone-- there was a big American PX.
You know the PXs?
And the captain of the PX, who was in charge of the PX-- and he had an assistant, a little Jewish fellow, Schloimela.
And Schloimela was selling cigarettes by the boxes, not by 50 cartons.
So we got through Schloimela to the captain, we started buying weapons.
The captain could sell anything.
He was in charge of the PX.
He had everything we wanted.
He was-- he was like a Walmart.
It was a little complicated.
My mother in law-- eventually, I got married.
My mother-in-law had some relatives here in the United States, and she was sent papers from the United States to these relatives.
And my wife, who was her daughter, wanted to go with her mother.
So this overruled my choices.
Nevertheless, I have never chosen-- really, to be honest, I have never chosen to go to Israel.
I think I-- I think I was selfish.
I thought, life in Israel would be hard.
I had enough hard life.
I wished to have something which is more normal.
This was not really the first time.
I missed something which I didn't tell you-- but yes, I remember that.
There was a DC-6 four-engine, propeller-driven plane.
We went from Bremerhaven in Germany to Glasgow, Scotland.
We stayed, I think, overnight.
From Glasgow, we went to Newfoundland, which is the closest distance between the two continents over the ocean.
And from Newfoundland, we went to New York.
And eventually-- we-- I-- we spent two weeks in New York, and then was shipped to Philadelphia.
Yes.
And the baby was in front of her.
The plane had special things like-- you have now-- you have now tables coming out here.
There were no tables at that time.
They used to bring tables and stick them into the seat.
They used to-- the stewardess used to bring them.
But for her, they brought like a, like a basket for a baby with the things.
And she was sitting here.
And the baby was right in front of her.
Good.
Switch tape, please.
